name: Borobudur
shortDescription:
Central Java, Indonesia Borobudur is the world's largest Buddhist monument and one of the greatest architectural achievements in human history.
                Built during the 8th-9th centuries CE (c
longDescription:
Central Java, Indonesia Borobudur is the world's largest Buddhist monument and one of the greatest architectural achievements in human history.
                Built during the 8th-9th centuries CE (c. 750-825 CE) during the reign of the Sailendra Dynasty ("Lords of the Mountain"),
                this colossal stone structure represents the pinnacle of Mahayana Buddhist architecture in Southeast Asia. The monument
                consists of nine stacked platforms—six square and three circular—topped by a central dome, all constructed from approximately
                2 million blocks of volcanic andesite stone quarried from nearby rivers. The entire structure is decorated with 2,672
                relief panels and originally contained 504 Buddha statues, creating a monumental three-dimensional mandala that physically
                embodies the Buddhist path to enlightenment.
description:
Explore temples, mountains, groves, and holy sites that have served as gateways between the human and divine realms
                across cultures and millennia. From ancient stone circles to living pilgrimage destinations, discover the places
                where heaven touches earth.
significance: Central Java, Indonesia Borobudur is the world's largest Buddhist monument and one of the greatest architectural achievements in human history. Built during the 8th-9th centuries CE (c.

geography: Located in Central Java, Indonesia (Magelang Regency). Covers approximately 2.5 hectares. Built in the 8th century. Nine-tiered structure (five square bases and four circular terraces); 506 Buddha statues and 2,672 relief panels; mandala-shaped when viewed from above
relatedEvents: Gradual abandonment due to volcanic eruptions
Rediscovery and restoration in modern times
Annual pilgrimages and Vesak (Buddha's Birthday) celebrations

keyMyths.title: The Cosmic Mountain
keyMyths.description: Borobudur's structure is a three-dimensional mandala representing the Buddhist cosmology. The monument symbolizes Mount Meru, the sacred mountain considered the center of the universe in Buddhist and Hindu traditions, and the journey to enlightenment.
keyMyths.source: Various Buddhist texts, including the Mahayana Sutras and cosmological treatises.

keyMyths.title: The Reliefs of the Karmawibhangga
keyMyths.description: Hidden at the base of Borobudur and later revealed, the Karmawibhangga reliefs depict scenes of cause and effect, illustrating the principle of Karma. These reliefs show the consequences of good and bad deeds in past lives, guiding practitioners towards ethical conduct.
keyMyths.source: Analysis of the Karmawibhangga reliefs at Borobudur and related commentaries on the law of Karma.

keyMyths.title: The Jataka Tales
keyMyths.description: Many of the reliefs on Borobudur depict the Jataka tales, stories of the Buddha's previous lives. These stories illustrate the virtues of compassion, wisdom, and self-sacrifice that the Buddha cultivated over countless lifetimes, serving as moral lessons for devotees.
keyMyths.source: The Jataka Tales, a collection of stories about the previous lives of the Buddha, found in the Pali Canon and other Buddhist scriptures.

keyMyths.title: The Gandavyuha Sutra
keyMyths.description: The upper levels of Borobudur, particularly the galleries leading to the stupas, depict scenes from the Gandavyuha Sutra. This sutra tells the story of Sudhana's pilgrimage and his encounters with various bodhisattvas, highlighting the importance of spiritual guidance and the interconnectedness of all beings.
keyMyths.source: The Gandavyuha Sutra, a major Mahayana Buddhist text, especially its depiction in the reliefs of Borobudur.

keyMyths.title: The Enlightenment of the Buddha (Nirvana)
keyMyths.description: The topmost stupa of Borobudur symbolizes Nirvana, the ultimate state of enlightenment and liberation from suffering. The journey to the top, from the base reliefs to the empty stupa, represents the spiritual path towards achieving Nirvana and transcending the cycle of rebirth.
keyMyths.source: Interpretations of Borobudur's architecture and symbolism in relation to Buddhist concepts of Nirvana and enlightenment.

sources.name: Jacques Dumarçay, Borobudur

sources.title: Jacques Dumarçay, Borobudur

sources.title: Jan Fontein, The Sculpture of Indonesia

sources.name: N.J. Krom, Barabudur: Archaeological Description

sources.title: N.J. Krom, Barabudur: Archaeological Description

sources.name: Pali Canon (various sutras)

sources.title: Pali Canon (various sutras)

sources.description: Mahayana Buddhist text whose story is depicted in reliefs at Borobudur.
